title: Open-Source vs Closed-Source Models
lesson:
Every time you build something with AI, one of the first forks in the road is this: do you use a closed-source model or an open-source one? It sounds like a licensing footnote, but it quietly shapes your cost, your privacy, your control, and even where your product can run. Let's make the difference concrete.

What "the model" actually is

Remember that a model is really just a giant pile of numbers called Parameters (the learned weights that make it behave). A closed-source model means the company keeps that pile of numbers locked away on their own servers. You never see the weights. You send text in over the internet, you get text back, and you pay per token. Think OpenAI's GPT line, Anthropic's Claude, or Google's Gemini. An open-weights model means the company publishes the actual numbers for you to download. You can run it on your own machine, inspect it, change it, and nobody has to know you used it. Think Meta's Llama, Mistral, Alibaba's Qwen, or DeepSeek.

💡 A note on words: most "open" models are technically open-weights, not fully open-source. You get the finished weights, but often not the secret training data or the exact recipe used to make them. True open-source (weights + data + code, all freely usable) is rarer. People say "open-source" loosely; in practice they almost always mean open-weights.

The restaurant vs home-kitchen analogy

A closed model is like eating at a great restaurant. The kitchen is hidden, the chef is world-class, you just order and a finished dish arrives. It's effortless and the quality is high — but you can't see the recipe, you can't cook it at 3am when they're shut, and every plate costs money. An open-weights model is like being handed the recipe and ingredients to cook at home. More work, you need a decent stove (a GPU — the specialised chip that runs these models fast), and the first few attempts may be clumsy. But it's yours: cook it anytime, tweak the seasoning, and never pay the restaurant again.

The five trade-offs that actually matter

Closed-source (API)Open-weights (self-host)
Best raw quality, usually a step aheadCatching up fast; top open models rival last year's closed ones
Easiest to start — one API callYou manage the server, GPU, and updates
Pay per token, foreverPay for hardware once (or rent it); cheap at high volume
Your data leaves your wallsData can stay fully on your machines
Can't fine-tune deeply or change behaviour at the weights levelFull control: fine-tune, quantize, edit freely

Calling a closed model is genuinely just a network request:

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()  # your secret API key, billed per token
reply = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-5",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise this contract."}],
)
print(reply.choices[0].message.content)

Running an open model on your own laptop is a different shape of work, but barely more code with the right tools:

# Download and run an open-weights model locally
ollama run llama3.1:8b "Summarise this contract."

That second command pulls the actual weights to your disk and runs them on your hardware. The contract text never touches anyone else's server — which is exactly why hospitals, banks, and law firms care. See Ollama and Hugging Face for where open models live and how to run them.

The library-card analogy for cost

Closed APIs are like paying per photocopy at a library: cheap if you copy ten pages, brutal if you copy a million. Open models are like buying the printer: a painful one-off cost, then near-free copies forever. So the rule of thumb is volume. Low or spiky traffic, or you want the absolute best quality? Closed wins. Huge steady volume, strict privacy, or a need to deeply customise the model? Open wins. Many real products use both: a cheap open model for the easy 90% of requests, and a closed model for the hard 10%.

Why open models unlock fine-tuning

Here's the part that matters for the rest of this course. You can only do real Fine-Tuning — actually reshaping the weights with cheap methods like LoRA — when you can hold the weights. Closed providers offer a limited, hosted fine-tuning service, but you can't download the result, run it offline, or shrink it with Quantization to fit a small GPU. If your goal is a custom model that's truly yours, open-weights isn't just an option — it's the whole reason this course exists.
mentalModel: Closed model = eating at a hidden-kitchen restaurant (effortless, top quality, pay every plate, can't see the recipe). Open model = cooking the same dish at home from the recipe (more setup, but it's yours to run, tweak, and serve forever).
mistakes: Calling open-weights models "open-source". You almost always get the finished weights, not the training data or full recipe. It's a meaningful difference — true open-source is rare, so say "open-weights" when you mean it.

Assuming open models are free. The download is free, but running them isn't — you pay for the GPU, electricity, and the engineer's time to keep the server alive. "Free" only beats an API at high, steady volume.

Picking closed-source while handling sensitive data without checking the contract. Sending medical or legal text to an external API may break privacy rules. Read the data-handling terms, or keep it in-house with an open model.

Believing open models are always far behind. The gap is real at the very top, but small. For many everyday tasks a good open model is plenty, and far cheaper at scale — test before assuming you need the priciest closed option.

Ignoring the licence on an open model. "Open" does not mean "do anything". Some licences restrict commercial use or huge-scale deployment. Always read the licence before shipping a product on top of one.

Treating it as one permanent choice. You can route easy requests to a cheap open model and hard ones to a closed one. Locking yourself to a single provider's API also makes switching later painful and expensive.
exercise:
Pick one realistic app idea — say, a tool that summarises customer support emails. Write a short decision note answering four questions: (1) Roughly how many requests per day will it handle? (2) Does the email content count as sensitive data? (3) Do you need the model to be the absolute smartest, or is "good enough" fine? (4) Will you ever want to fine-tune it on your own examples? Based on your answers, decide: closed API, self-hosted open model, or a mix — and write one sentence justifying it.

Stretch goal: Put rough numbers on it. Assume 50,000 requests a day at about 800 tokens each. Estimate the monthly bill at a closed-API price of roughly $2 per million tokens, then compare that to renting a single cloud GPU for a month (ballpark $1,000–$1,500). Which wins, and at what daily volume does the answer flip?
solution: For the support-email tool, the content is often sensitive (customer details) and the volume is high and steady — both arrows point toward a self-hosted open model, possibly with a closed model as a fallback for tricky escalations. On the numbers: 50,000 requests x 800 tokens ≈ 40 million tokens/day ≈ 1.2 billion/month. At $2 per million that's roughly $2,400/month for the API — clearly more than ~$1,200 to rent one GPU, so open self-hosting wins here. The flip point is where API cost drops below GPU rental: somewhere around a few million tokens per day. Below that, the API's zero-maintenance simplicity usually wins; above it, owning the hardware pays off. Exact figures shift with prices, but the shape of the trade-off is the durable lesson.
summary: Closed-source models live on the maker's servers — you call them over an API, pay per token, get top quality, but can't see or change the weights. Open-weights models can be downloaded and run on your own hardware: more setup, but full control, on-prem privacy, cheaper at high volume, and the ability to truly fine-tune. Choose by volume, privacy needs, quality bar, and whether you want to customise — and remember "open" usually means open-weights, not fully open-source.

keyTerms.def: A model whose weights are kept private. You access it only through the maker's API and pay per token; you never download or inspect it. e.g. GPT, Claude, Gemini.
A model whose learned weights are published for download. You can run it on your own hardware, inspect it, fine-tune it, and quantize it. e.g. Llama, Mistral, Qwen.
Truly open-source means weights, training data, and code are all freely shared. Most "open" models share only the finished weights, so "open-weights" is the precise term.
A way to use a model over the internet: you send a request with your text and key, the provider runs the model on their servers and returns the answer.
Running a model on hardware you own or rent, rather than calling someone else's service. Gives you privacy and control but you handle setup and upkeep.
The pile of learned numbers that define how a model behaves. Owning them (open-weights) is what lets you run a model offline and fine-tune it.
Keeping data and models inside your own walls so sensitive information never leaves to an outside provider — a key reason regulated industries pick open models.
The legal terms attached to a model. "Open" does not always mean unrestricted; some licences limit commercial or very-large-scale use, so read it before shipping.
